content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

February 28, 2001


Richard Rothman
115 Dolores Street # 5
San Francisco, CA 94103

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-00-260

Dear Mr. Rothman:

This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the Raoul Wallenberg
Jewish Democratic Club ("club") regarding the slate mailer provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. The club is a slate mailer organization. Last year, the club paid for two newspaper
advertisements publicizing the club's endorsement of several candidates and ballot measures,
some of which paid to be included in the ad. Does the Act require the club to report this
transaction on its slate mailer organization campaign statement?

2. If the club's slate mailers support candidates for the State Legislature, does the Act
require the club to file its campaign statements with Los Angeles County?

CONCLUSIONS

1. A portion of each newspaper ad resulted in the club making independent expenditures
for those state and local candidates and ballot measures that did not pay to be included in the ads
and were not consulted before the club paid for the ads. If these expenditures total $1,000 or
more, the club must disclose these payments on its state mailer organization campaign statement.

2. Yes. Slate mailer organizations that produce slate mailers supporting or opposing
state candidates must file in the same manner as state general purpose committees.

___________________________
¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections 18109-
18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-00-260
Page No. 2

FACTS

The Raoul Wallenberg Jewish Democratic Club is one of the largest political clubs in the
Bay Area. The club promotes Jewish candidates and Jewish issues and plays an active role in the
policies of the Democratic Party. Club events address anti-Semitism, welfare, the religious right,
and crisis in education. The club also publishes a monthly newsletter and holds an annual dinner
honoring outstanding San Franciscans. The club collects membership dues that help in the
operations of the club but do not fund political activities.

The club is also a slate mailer organization. It receives money from candidates and ballot
measure committees who wish to appear in the club's slate mailer. Last year, when a candidate
or committee paid to be in the club's slate mailer, the candidate or committee also paid to appear
in the club's newspaper ad, which listed all of the candidates and measures endorsed by the club.
The political ad appeared in the Jewish bulletin, a local newspaper. Only some of the candidates
and measures that appeared in the club's slate mailer and newspaper ad actually paid the club for
the publication.

Last year, the club purchased two newspaper ads, one for the primary election and one
for the general election. The cost of each ad was approximately $800. None of the paying
candidates and committees specifically earmarked their payment for the inclusion of any non-
paying candidate or committee. However, at the time of payment, the paying candidates and
committees knew which candidates and ballot measures the club intended to endorse.

ANALYSIS

A. Committees and Slate Mailer Organizations

Committees and slate mailer organizations must file campaign statements that disclose
their financial activities. In general, a "slate mailer organization" is a person that produces slate
mailers, exercises control over the subject matter in slate mailers, and receives $500 or more in a
calendar year for the production of slate mailers. (Section 82048.4.) A "slate mailer" is a mass
mailing (200 substantially similar pieces of mail) that supports or opposes a total of four or more
candidates or ballot measures. (Sections 82041.5, 82048.3.)

On its campaign statements, a slate mailer organization must disclose only those
payments that the organization receives and spends for the production and distribution of slate
mailers. (Section 84219.) However, when a slate mailer organization qualifies as a committee,
it must also report its contributions and expenditures.

A committee is a person or group of persons that, in a calendar year, receives
contributions of $1,000 or more ("recipient committee"), makes independent expenditures of
$1,000 or more ("independent-expenditure committee"), or makes contributions of $10,000 or
more ("major-donor committee"). (Section 82013.) A "contribution" is a payment made for

File No. A-00-260
Page No. 3

political purposes except to the extent that the person making the payment receives full and
adequate consideration. (Section 82015, Reg. 18215.) An "independent expenditure" includes
payments made in connection with a communication that expressly advocates the election or
defeat of a clearly identified candidate that is not made in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with that candidate. (Section 82031, Reg. 18225.7.)

1. Payments Made for Slate Mailers

The Act expressly provides that payments made by a slate mailer organization to produce
and distribute slate mailers are not contributions or independent expenditures. (Section
82048.4.) In addition, we have advised that a payment made by a candidate to a slate mailer
organization for the candidate's appearance in a slate mailer is not a contribution since the
candidate is presumably receiving full and adequate consideration. (Section 82015(a); Finley
Advice Letter, No. A-92-219.)

Similarly, when a candidate pays for space in a mailer that will include a non-paying
candidate, no contribution or independent expenditure results because the inclusion of the non-
paying candidate is usually for the benefit of the paying candidate. (Remcho Advice Letter, No.
A-86-223.) However, when candidates pay more than their pro rata share for a slate mailer, and
specifically earmark their inflated payment for the inclusion of other candidates, the paying
candidates are making contributions or independent expenditures relative to the other candidates.
(Sivesind Advice Letter, No. I-96-279.)

2. The Club's Transactions

Last year, a handful of candidates and ballot measure committees paid the club to appear
in the club's primary and general election slate mailer and the club's primary and general
election newspaper advertisements. The slate mailers and newspaper ads endorsed several
other candidates and ballot measures that did not pay the club for the endorsements. None of the
paying candidates and committees specifically earmarked their payment for any other candidate
or committee. However, at the time of payment, the paying candidates and committees knew
which candidates and ballot measures the club intended to endorse.

You would like to know whether these transactions resulted in reportable contributions or
independent expenditures. As to the slate mailers, the paying candidates and committees
presumably received full and adequate consideration and therefore did not make reportable
contributions to the club. (Finley Advice Letter, supra.) In addition, the club's endorsements in
the slate mailers of non-paying candidates and ballot measures did not result in independent
expenditures by the club because the Act provides a specific exception for such activity.
(Section 82048.2(c).)

With respect to the newspaper ads, the first issue is whether the paying candidates and
committees received full and adequate consideration in exchange for their payment to appear in

File No. A-00-260
Page No. 4

the newspaper ads, which also endorsed non-paying candidates and ballot measures. Arguably,
the paying candidates and committees did not receive full and adequate consideration because
they had to "share" advertisement space with the non-paying candidates. On the
other hand, one could argue that the inclusion of the non-paying candidates, which include
nominees and incumbents for high-ranking offices, actually benefit the paying candidates whose
names may not be as familiar to the voters. In the slate mailer context, we have adopted the
second view that inclusion of non-paying candidates benefits the paying candidates. (Remcho,
supra.) This rationale applies equally well to the club's newspaper ads. Therefore, we conclude
that the paying candidates and committees did not make political contributions when they paid to
appear in the club's newspaper ads.

The second issue is whether the club made reportable payments to the non-paying
candidates and ballot measures by including them in the newspaper ads free of charge. Unlike
slate mailers, newspaper advertisements purchased by slate mailer organizations are not exempt
from regulation as contributions or independent expenditures. Therefore, if the club placed
ads without consulting an endorsed non-paying candidate or ballot measure committee, then the
club made independent expenditures for that candidate or committee.²

The club will qualify as an independent expenditure committee if these independent
expenditures reached $1,000 or more in a calendar year. (Section 82013(b).) According to your
facts, the total cost of the two newspaper ads purchased by the club in the year 2000 was
approximately $1,600. To determine how much of the $1,600 is attributable to independent
expenditures, you must divide $1,600 by the total number of candidates and committees
(including federal candidates) that the club endorsed. Then multiply that figure by the number of
non-paying state and local candidates and ballot measures that the club did not consult.³ If the
sum equals $1,000 or more, the club must report those independent expenditures.

When a slate mailer organization becomes an independent expenditure committee, the
Act does not require the organization to file separately as a committee, as long as the
organization reports the independent expenditures on its slate mailer organization campaign
statement. (Sections 82048.5, 84219(k).)

B. Where to File Campaign Statements

A slate mailer organization must file its campaign reports with the clerk of the county in
which it is domiciled. (Section 84218(c).) The Club's domicile is San Francisco County. In
addition, those slate mailer organizations that produce slate mailers supporting or opposing state

___________
² If the club consulted the non-paying candidates and committees beforehand, then the club would have
made non-monetary (or in-kind) contributions to the non-paying candidates or committees. The club would become
a major donor committee only if the in-kind contributions totaled $10,000 or more in a calendar year. (Section
82013(c).)
³ If the club endorsed the same non-paying candidate or ballot measure in both newspaper ads, it must
count that candidate or committee twice for purposes of determining how much of the $1,600 is attributable to
independent expenditures made by the club.

File No. A-00-260
Page No. 5

candidates must file their campaign statements in the same manner as state general purpose
committees. (Section 84218(c)(1).)

A state general purpose committee must file its campaign statements as follows: (1) the
original and one copy with the Secretary of State; (2) two copies with the Registrar-Recorder of
Los Angeles County; and (3) two copies with the Registrar of Voters of the City and County of
San Francisco. (Section 84215(a).) The Club produces slate mailers that support or oppose
candidates for state office. Accordingly, it must file its campaign statements in the same manner
as state general-purpose committees.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Julia Bilaver
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

sections.conclusion:
1. A portion of each newspaper ad resulted in the club making independent expenditures
for those state and local candidates and ballot merrures that did not pay to be included in the ads
and were not consulted before the club paid for the ads. If these expenditures total $1,000 or
more, the club must disclose these payments on its slate mailer organization campaign statement.
2. Yes. Slate mailer orgarizations that produce slate mailers supporting or opposing
state candidates must file in the same manner as state general purpose committees.

CONCLUSION: 1. A portion of each newspaper ad resulted in the club making independent expenditures
for those state and local candidates and ballot merrures that did not pay to be included in the ads
and were not consulted before the club paid for the ads. If these expenditures total $1,000 or
more, the club must disclose these payments on its slate mailer organization campaign statement.
2. Yes. Slate mailer orgarizations that produce slate mailers supporting or opposing
state candidates must file in the same manner as state general purpose committees.
